sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 67-year-old male, presented with a 4-day history of right shoulder pain that started after a tackle football injury.
He reports that he landed on his right shoulder during the game and has been experiencing pain in the posterior aspect of his right shoulder, specifically around the acromioclavicular joint.
He denies numbness or tingling in his fingers and has no problems with strength in his arm.
He is able to lift his arm but experiences pain when trying to abduct or adduct his shoulder.
He has taken Tylenol for the pain but it has not provided significant relief.
He reports a slight headache but no fever or chills.
He has a past medical history of appendicitis and an appendectomy 3-4 years ago.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of appendicitis and an appendectomy 3-4 years ago.
Social history: The patient is a 67-year-old male who participates in tackle football.
Review of systems: The patient denies any neck problems, elbow pain, or wrist pain.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, heart rate 82 beats per minute, oxygen saturation 95% on room air.
Physical exam: The cervical spine exam is normal with no tenderness to palpation.
The head exam shows no trauma, ecchymosis, or bruising on the right shoulder.

The musculoskeletal exam reveals pain to palpation of the posterior shoulder at the acromioclavicular joint, decreased abduction and adduction to 90 degrees, pain with supination and pronation of the right arm, and a palpable radial artery pulse.
Test Results: The right shoulder x-ray shows an acute acromioclavicular joint fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an acute acromioclavicular joint fracture.
Problem list: Acromioclavicular joint fracture; suspected orthopedic injury.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 15 mg once daily for pain management.
I ordered a CT scan of the right shoulder to further evaluate the joint.
I referred the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the shoulder and avoid future surgery.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again to follow up on the CT scan results and to assess the healing progress of the fracture.
